one_line_takeaway: AGDI generates adversarially-optimized trigger images that jointly exploit response-level (cross-entropy) and semantic-level (CLIP-embedding) alignment, plus min-max adversarial training against a simulated fine-tuned model, to achieve black-box copyright/ownership verification of MLLM derivatives that is substantially more robust to fine-tuning, pruning, merging, and quantization than prior trigger-based baselines (PLA, RNA, IF), while maintaining 0% false-positive ASR on unrelated non-derivative models.
key_finding: On Qwen2-VL derivatives (Table 2), AGDI achieves ASR of 53/77/41/81/68% (LoRA) and 46/65/33/80/45% (full fine-tuning) on V7W/ST-VQA/TextVQA/PaintingF/MathV respectively, versus PLA's 48/68/33/76/60% (LoRA) and 43/60/28/75/38% (full), consistently outperforming PLA, RNA, and Ordinary. On LLaVA-1.5 LoRA derivatives (Table 3), AGDI reaches 64/56/36/79/30% versus PLA's 51/43/21/55/18%, IF's 28/22/30/8/24%, and RNA's 28/31/14/20/9%. On non-derivative models (MiniGPT-4, Qwen2-VL, LLaVA-1.6, Llama3-Vision) triggers built from LLaVA-1.5, AGDI, PLA, and RNA all achieve 0% ASR (Table 4), i.e., no false positives. Under model pruning on LLaVA-1.5 derivatives (Table 5), AGDI substantially outperforms PLA/RNA at every sparsity level, e.g., PaintingF at 10% magnitude pruning: AGDI 79% vs PLA 46% vs RNA 20%. Under model merging (Table 6) and 8-bit quantization (Figure 4), AGDI again dominates the two baselines. Ablation (Table 7) shows removing response-level injection collapses ASR to near 0% (0-4%) across all datasets, showing it is the primary driver of activation, while removing semantic-level injection (using plain cross-entropy) drops performance to PLA-like levels (e.g., LLaVA-1.5 V7W: 51% vs full AGDI 64%), and removing the adversarial-training components ('w/o LLM update', 'w/o encoder update') also reduces ASR versus full AGDI.
core_contribution: The paper proposes AGDI (Adversarial-Guided Dual-Injection), a black-box copyright tracking method for multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Given only black-box access to a base model, the publisher optimizes a 'trigger image' (via PGD-style perturbation of a clean image, budget epsilon=16/255) so that a fixed rare trigger question elicits a fixed target answer. Two loss terms are jointly optimized: (1) a response-level injection Lres, a cross-entropy loss forcing the auxiliary MLLM's output to match the target answer text, and (2) a semantic-level injection Lsem, a CLIP cosine-similarity loss aligning the image's CLIP embedding with the target text's CLIP embedding, exploiting the observation that MLLMs' CLIP-like cross-modal alignment module stays relatively stable under downstream fine-tuning. On top of this dual-injection objective, the authors add a min-max adversarial training loop (Algorithm 1) in which an auxiliary copy of the model's parameters is updated to actively suppress the target response (simulating what a fine-tuned derivative might do), while the trigger image is simultaneously updated to remain effective against that adversarial model, with parameters reset from a reference checkpoint after each trigger's optimization to avoid cumulative drift.
threat_model: Explicitly defined in the paper (Section 3.1). Two parties: (1) Publishers release an open-source base MLLM and later want to verify, via black-box querying only (no access to internals/gradients of the suspect model, no knowledge of the fine-tuning data or process used by third parties), whether a suspicious deployed model is a derivative of their released model. (2) Malicious users have white-box access to the released base model's parameters and can fine-tune it (LoRA or full fine-tuning) on arbitrary private downstream data to build proprietary derivatives, but operate under a limited compute budget that precludes full retraining from scratch (which would erase the base model's value). The verification condition (Eq. 1) requires the trigger to make both the base model and its fine-tuned derivatives answer the target text, while non-derivative (unrelated) models must not.
limitations: The paper's own text does not include an explicit dedicated limitations section (not found in the extracted body or conclusion). Notable caveats implicit in the setup: the method is evaluated only on two base MLLMs (LLaVA-1.5-7B, Qwen2-VL-2B) and requires white-box access to the base model at trigger-construction time even though verification itself is black-box; the malicious-user threat model explicitly excludes adversaries with large compute budgets who could fully retrain the model; ASR detection uses exact/substring string matching against short target phrases, which could be sensitive to phrasing choices; and the paper does not report false-positive/false-negative rates under adaptive adversaries who are aware of the AGDI trigger-construction technique itself (only pruning, merging, and quantization are tested as evasion strategies, not trigger-detection or trigger-removal-aware fine-tuning).

datasets_used: ImageNet-1K validation set (200 clean images for trigger construction)
quantitative_results: Qwen2-VL LoRA fine-tuning ASR (V7W/ST-VQA/TextVQA/PaintingF/MathV): AGDI 53/77/41/81/68% vs PLA 48/68/33/76/60% vs RNA 36/39/22/40/37% vs Ordinary 36/46/22/48/41%
Qwen2-VL full fine-tuning ASR: AGDI 46/65/33/80/45% vs PLA 43/60/28/75/38%
Non-derivative model false-positive ASR (MiniGPT-4, Qwen2-VL, LLaVA-1.6, Llama3-V, triggers from LLaVA-1.5): AGDI/PLA/RNA all 0%
Pruning robustness on PaintingF (LLaVA-1.5, magnitude pruning 10%): AGDI 79% vs PLA 46% vs RNA 20%
Ablation: removing response-level injection collapses ASR to 0-4% across all five datasets on both base models; removing semantic-level injection drops LLaVA-1.5 V7W ASR from 64% (full AGDI) to 51%

Utility check: LoRA-fine-tuned LLaVA-1.5 accuracy rises after fine-tuning (e.g., V7W 0.6% before -> 37.9% after; MathV 27.1% before -> 57.5% after), confirming derivative models remain functionally useful, not degraded by trigger injection
baselines_compared: Ordinary (vanilla cross-entropy trigger optimization, frozen model)
PLA / Parameter Learning Attack (Wang et al., ICLR 2025)
IF / Instructional Fingerprinting (Xu et al., NAACL 2024)
